extracted_information: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is a Linux kernel feature used to restrict actions within a container by operating on the calling process's seccomp state, limiting application access to system calls. It is available if Docker is built with seccomp and the kernel has `CONFIG_SECCOMP` enabled. Docker uses seccomp to run containers with least privilege.

specifications.default_profile_action: `SCMP_ACT_ERRNO` (Permission Denied) for default, `SCMP_ACT_ALLOW` for specific system calls. Some rules allow variants of system calls with specific arguments.

features.name: Default Seccomp Profile
features.description: Provides a sane default for running containers, disabling approximately 44 out of 300+ system calls. It's moderately protective while offering wide application compatibility. It functions as an allowlist, denying access by default and explicitly allowing specific system calls. It is not recommended to change this default profile.

features.name: System Call Filtering Mechanism
features.description: The profile defines a `defaultAction` of `SCMP_ACT_ERRNO` (causing a 'Permission Denied' error) and then overrides this action to `SCMP_ACT_ALLOW` for a specific list of system calls. Some rules allow variants of system calls with specific arguments.

features.name: Custom Profile Application
features.description: Users can override the default profile by explicitly specifying a custom profile using the `--security-opt` option when running a container. To run without any seccomp profile, `unconfined` can be passed.
features.disable_command_example: docker run --rm -it --security-opt seccomp=unconfined debian:latest unshare --map-root-user --user sh -c whoami

system_calls_blocked_by_default_profile.description: Accounting syscall which could let containers disable their own resource limits or process accounting. Also gated by `CAP_SYS_PACCT`.
Prevent containers from using the kernel keyring, which is not namespaced.
Deny loading potentially persistent BPF programs into kernel, already gated by `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`.
Time/date is not namespaced. Also gated by `CAP_SYS_TIME`.
Time/date is not namespaced. Also gated by `CAP_SYS_TIME`.
Deny cloning new namespaces. Also gated by `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` for CLONE_* flags, except `CLONE_NEWUSER`.

Tracing/profiling syscall, which could leak a lot of information on the host.
Prevent container from enabling BSD emulation. Not inherently dangerous, but poorly tested, potential for a lot of kernel vulnerabilities.
Deny `pivot_root`, should be privileged operation.

Restrict process inspection capabilities, already blocked by dropping `CAP_SYS_PTRACE`.

Tracing/profiling syscall. Blocked in Linux kernel versions before 4.8 to avoid seccomp bypass. Tracing/profiling arbitrary processes is already blocked by dropping `CAP_SYS_PTRACE`, because it could leak a lot of information on the host.
Deny manipulation and functions on kernel modules. Obsolete.
Quota syscall which could let containers disable their own resource limits or process accounting. Also gated by `CAP_SYS_ADMIN`.
Don't let containers reboot the host. Also gated by `CAP_SYS_BOOT`.
